completeness.rationale: Five of seven expected items were captured: linear memory growth (~500MB to 4GB+), v2.3 upgrade trigger (~1 week ago), ~200 active users, consistent day-to-day pattern, and restart as workaround. Two items were missed: server specs (8GB RAM VM) were never asked about, and the v2.3 changelog mention of notification system changes was listed as a future action for a developer rather than extracted from the reporter.

accuracy.rationale: The triage correctly identifies the v2.3 upgrade as the regression trigger and explicitly names 'event listeners registered per-request but never removed' as the leading hypothesis — which is precisely the actual cause. This satisfies acceptable path #2 (per-request resource leak pattern) and partially satisfies path #1 (v2.3 as trigger, but without pinpointing the notification/WebSocket subsystem specifically). The recommended fix steps would lead a developer directly to the bug. No significant inaccuracies present.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent resolved after only two questions. Both were excellent, but asking about the v2.3 changelog was an obvious and high-value follow-up that was skipped — it would have surfaced the 'improved real-time notifications' entry and pointed directly at the subsystem. Server hardware specs were also never elicited. The agent had a reasonable basis to resolve but left at least one clear diagnostic avenue unexplored.

economy.rationale: Both questions were essential and well-sequenced. No redundant turns, no information re-asked, no padding. The conversation moved directly from symptom to root cause hypothesis in two exchanges.

question_quality.rationale: Both questions demonstrated genuine diagnostic reasoning. Q1 (recent/gradual/always?) immediately surfaced the v2.3 upgrade correlation — information the reporter hadn't volunteered in the issue body. Q2 (activity-correlated vs. steady?) ruled out background process leaks and confirmed the per-request nature of the leak, meaningfully narrowing the search space. Both questions were well-framed with multiple-choice options to reduce friction.

actionability.rationale: The summary provides specific reproduction steps, a prioritized root cause hypothesis naming event listeners as the most likely culprit, a concrete fix workflow (diff v2.2→v2.3, heap profile under load, look for unbounded caches/listeners/connections), and a proposed soak test. A developer could begin the investigation immediately without gathering additional context.

notable_strengths: Exceptional efficiency: resolved with only 2 turns, both highly targeted
Root cause hypothesis explicitly named 'event listeners registered per-request but never removed' — the actual cause — as the leading candidate
Q1 elicited the v2.3 upgrade correlation that the reporter had not volunteered in the original issue
Q2 ruled out background/GC processes and confirmed per-request leak pattern, meaningfully constraining the search
Recommended fix steps are specific and correctly prioritized (diff + heap profiler + specific patterns to look for)
notable_weaknesses: Never asked about server specs (8GB RAM VM) — a standard environmental detail useful for severity assessment and profiling guidance
Did not ask the reporter to check the v2.3 changelog, which would have surfaced 'improved real-time notifications' and directly implicated the WebSocket subsystem
The notification/WebSocket system was not identified as the specific subsystem, only inferred generically as 'request-handling code paths'
most_insightful_question: Does the memory growth correlate with user activity, or does it climb steadily even during quiet periods? — This question ruled out background process leaks and timer-based accumulation, confirming the per-request nature of the leak and substantially narrowing the investigation scope.
missed_opportunities: Ask the reporter to check the v2.3 changelog for what changed in the notification or real-time features — this would have directly surfaced the notification system as the likely subsystem
Ask about server hardware specs (total RAM, CPU) to contextualize severity and inform profiling recommendations
Ask whether any new v2.3 features (e.g., real-time notifications, WebSocket-based updates) were being actively used, which could have pinpointed the subsystem without needing the changelog
